md: ### A triangle that's mostly holes
Take an equilateral triangle. Find the midpoints of each side, connect them — that splits the original into four smaller triangles. Now remove the middle (upside-down) one.
Repeat with each of the three remaining triangles: split into four, remove the middle. Then repeat again. And again. And again.
Forever.
What's left is the **Sierpinski triangle**, named after the Polish mathematician Wacław Sierpinski (1882–1969). It is the prototypical **fractal** — a shape with infinite detail at every scale.

Drag the **Iterations** slider. At iteration 0 you see the original solid triangle. At iteration 1, the middle has been removed. By iteration 6, the pattern is so fine that the picture looks like a delicate fabric.
Mathematically, the Sierpinski triangle is the *limit* — what you'd get if you could keep going forever.

blocks.title: Each iteration counts the pieces
blocks.body:
At iteration $n$:

- Total triangles: $3^n$
- Side length of each: $1/2^n$ of the original
- Total perimeter (of remaining pieces): $3 \cdot (3/2)^n$ — grows without bound!
- Total area: $(3/4)^n$ of original — shrinks to zero.

The Sierpinski triangle has **infinite perimeter and zero area**. Most of the original triangle has been removed, but the boundary is fractally complicated.

md: ### Three ways to build it
**(1) Removal.** Start solid; remove middle triangles forever (what we just did).
**(2) Three corner copies.** Start with one solid triangle. Make three smaller (half-size) copies at the three corners. Repeat with each.

**(3) Chaos game.** Start at a random point. Pick a random vertex of the triangle, move halfway toward it, plot a dot. Repeat thousands of times. The dots converge to fill out the Sierpinski triangle — even though the procedure looks random!
All three give exactly the same shape. That's a clue that the fractal has deep self-consistency.

blocks.title: Fractal dimension
blocks.body:
The Sierpinski triangle isn't 2-dimensional like a normal triangle. It's not 1-dimensional like a line either. It has **fractional dimension**:

$d = \dfrac{\log 3}{\log 2} \approx 1.585$

Intuition: at each scale, 1 piece is replaced by 3 pieces, each at half the size. The dimension is the number $d$ such that $3 = 2^d$, giving $d = \log_2 3 \approx 1.585$.

Fractal dimensions are what put the 'fractal' in 'fractal' (Benoit Mandelbrot, 1975).

md: ### It's hiding in Pascal's triangle!
Build Pascal's triangle (where each entry is the sum of the two above). Color each cell black if its value is odd, white if even.
**You get a Sierpinski triangle pattern.**
This is no coincidence: the parity of $\binom{n}{k}$ follows a recursive structure that exactly mirrors Sierpinski's construction. Look up 'Pascal's triangle mod 2' and prepare to be amazed.
### Why fractals matter
**Coastlines, mountains, clouds, lightning** — natural shapes that aren't smooth (so calculus struggles) but aren't random either (so simple statistics fail). They're fractal.
**Data compression** — fractal image compression exploits self-similarity to encode complex images compactly.
**Computer graphics** — landscape generation, plant rendering, texture synthesis. Random fractals make convincing trees, mountains, terrain.
**Mathematical physics** — phase transitions, percolation, turbulence. Many natural critical phenomena are fractal at their critical point.
Sierpinski's little triangle is a doorway into all of this.
